https://www.reactbd.com/Technical interviews never predicted job performance, and now 38% of candidates are AI-cheating them anyway. The evidence, the paid-work-trial replacement, and how both sides get ready.
Here’s the story that should end every argument about technical interviews, told by a man who spent 35 years conducting them. At Google, a site’s Hiring Committee — fifteen senior engineers, the final gatekeepers on every hire — was handed a batch of anonymized interview packets as a “calibration exercise.” They reviewed them the way they reviewed everything, and voted to reject about two-thirds. Then the recruiters revealed the trick: the packets were their own, from when each of them had interviewed at Google. The gatekeepers had voted to not hire most of themselves.
Steve Yegge — ex-Amazon Bar Raiser, ex-Google Hiring Committee, author of the screening guide Google recruiters still hand out — published that story this May in an essay declaring the technical interview dead. He’s right, and it’s worth understanding precisely why he’s right, because the evidence has actually been public for decades. What’s new in 2026 is the thing that finally made it impossible to ignore.
